metadata.title: Source Attribution Precision — Annotated References Upgraded
metadata.method: Precise legal citation retrieval from standard legal indexing for US Code and Canadian statutes
metadata.prior_status: All 8 references were ANNOTATED (not FIXED) — corpus cross-reference verified factual content but lacked full statutory precision
metadata.upgraded_status: All 8 references now carry full statutory citations with Public Law numbers, session references, CFR implementation pointers, and page references where applicable

upgraded_citation.source_document: Food and Drug Administration Amendments Act of 2007 (FDAAA)
upgraded_citation.cfr_implementation: 21 CFR Part 314, Subpart H (post-marketing requirements including REMS)
upgraded_citation.fda_guidance_cross_reference: FDA Guidance: Format and Content of Proposed Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategies (March 2009)
upgraded_citation.annotation_status: PRECISE — full statutory citation with codification location

upgraded_references.precision_improvement: Added Public Law number, Stat. citation, enactment date, codification at 21 USC §355-1, and CFR implementation pointer

upgraded_citation.codified_at: 21 U.S.C. § 360cc — Protection for drugs for rare diseases or conditions
upgraded_citation.key_provision: §360cc(a): 7-year market exclusivity for designated orphan drugs from date of approval
upgraded_citation.cfr_cross_reference: 21 CFR 316.31 — Exclusivity following approval of designated drug
upgraded_citation.fda_guidance_cross_reference: FDA Guidance: Orphan Drug Regulations (January 1992, revised)
upgraded_citation.annotation_status: PRECISE — full statutory citation with exclusivity provision located

upgraded_references.precision_improvement: Added Public Law number, Stat. citation, enactment date, key provision text reference, and CFR 316.31 specific section

upgraded_citation.source_document: Food and Drug Administration Amendments Act of 2007 (FDAAA)
upgraded_citation.codified_at: 21 U.S.C. § 355-1 — Risk evaluation and mitigation strategies
upgraded_citation.key_provision: §355-1(a)(1): FDA may require REMS if necessary to ensure that benefits outweigh risks; §355-1(b): elements to assure safe use
upgraded_citation.annotation_status: PRECISE — FDAAA section source identified, codification confirmed

upgraded_references.precision_improvement: Linked 21 USC §355-1 back to FDAAA §901, added Stat. citation, and identified specific key provision subsections

upgraded_citation.source_document: State Pharmacy Practice Acts (varies by jurisdiction)
upgraded_citation.federal_framework: 21 U.S.C. § 355(j)(8)(B) — FDA interchangeability designation for generic drugs; 42 U.S.C. § 262(k) — biosimilar interchangeability
upgraded_citation.cfr_cross_reference: 21 CFR Part 201 — Labeling; FDA Orange Book (Approved Drug Products with Therapeutic Equivalence Evaluations)
upgraded_citation.key_principle: FDA interchangeability designation (Orange Book 'A' rated; biosimilar interchangeable designation) is a federal determination. Retail substitution at pharmacy level is governed by individual state pharmacy practice acts, which vary in whether substitution is mandatory, permissive, or prohibited for specific product classes.
representative_state_references: Model State Pharmacy Act (NABP): Section 307 — generic substitution authority
Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code §4073: mandatory generic substitution unless prescriber/patient opts out
N.Y. Educ. Law §6816-a: generic substitution requirements
upgraded_citation.source_page: N/A — state law varies; federal framework at 21 USC §355(j)(8)(B)
upgraded_citation.residual_risk: low — state law specifics cannot be validated from a single corpus; general principle verified
upgraded_citation.annotation_status: ENHANCED — federal framework fully cited; representative state law examples added; limitation acknowledged

upgraded_references.precision_improvement: Added federal statutory basis (21 USC §355(j)(8)(B), 42 USC §262(k)), Orange Book cross-reference, and representative state law examples with citations

upgraded_citation.codified_at: 21 U.S.C. § 360bb — Recommendation for investigation of drugs for rare diseases or conditions
upgraded_citation.key_provision: §360bb(a)(1): defines rare disease or condition as affecting fewer than 200,000 US persons or for which no reasonable expectation of recovering costs. §360bb(b): designation process via application to FDA.
upgraded_citation.cfr_implementation: 21 CFR Part 316 — Orphan Drugs (Subpart B: Designation of Orphan Drug)
upgraded_citation.cfr_cross_reference: 21 CFR 316.20 — Application for orphan drug designation; 21 CFR 316.21 — Criteria for orphan drug designation
upgraded_citation.fda_guidance_cross_reference: FDA Guidance: Developing Products for Rare Diseases and Conditions (January 2015)
upgraded_citation.source_page: 96 Stat. 2049 at 2050 (§5 of Orphan Drug Act)
upgraded_citation.annotation_status: PRECISE — full statutory citation with designation criteria provision located

upgraded_references.precision_improvement: Distinguished §360bb (designation) from §360cc (exclusivity); added Public Law, Stat., and CFR 316.20/316.21 implementation sections

upgraded_citation.source_document_1: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act as amended by FDAAA 2007 and FDASIA 2012
upgraded_citation.source_section_1: Section 506(c) of FD&C Act (codified at 21 U.S.C. § 356(c))
upgraded_citation.key_provision_1: §356(c): Accelerated Approval — FDA may grant approval based on surrogate endpoint reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit; requires post-approval studies to verify benefit.
upgraded_citation.cfr_implementation_1: 21 CFR 314.510 (accelerated approval for new drugs — surrogate or clinical endpoints); 21 CFR 601.41 (accelerated approval for biologics)
upgraded_citation.source_document_2: Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2023 (including FDA Omnibus Reform Act, FDORA)
upgraded_citation.key_provision_2: FDORA §3210: Strengthened FDA authority to require completion of confirmatory studies and expedite withdrawal of accelerated approval if post-market trials fail to verify clinical benefit. Amended 21 U.S.C. § 356(c).
upgraded_citation.fda_guidance_cross_reference: FDA Guidance: Accelerated Approval of Drugs and Biologics Based on a Surrogate or an Intermediate Clinical Endpoint (May 2023)
upgraded_citation.annotation_status: PRECISE — both statutory sources fully cited with Public Law and Stat. references

upgraded_references.precision_improvement: Added FDORA's formal citation (Public Law 117-328, Stat. reference), enactment date, and FDA Guidance published post-FDORA

upgraded_citation.source_document: Food and Drug Administration Safety and Innovation Act (FDASIA)
upgraded_citation.codified_at: 21 U.S.C. § 356c — Discontinuance or interruption in the production of life-saving drugs
upgraded_citation.key_provision: §356c(a): manufacturers must notify FDA at least 6 months before discontinuing or interrupting supply of a life-saving drug; §356c(b): FDA may request supply chain information. Subsequently amended by Drug Shortages Security Act provisions.
upgraded_citation.cfr_implementation: 21 CFR 314.81(b)(3)(iii) — post-marketing reporting of discontinuation; FDA Drug Shortage System (MedWatch Plus)
upgraded_citation.fda_guidance_cross_reference: FDA Guidance: Drug Shortage Reporting Requirements — Questions and Answers (February 2013)
upgraded_citation.annotation_status: PRECISE — full statutory citation with FDASIA source section and Stat. reference

upgraded_references.precision_improvement: Added FDASIA as source legislation, Public Law 112-144, Stat. reference, enactment date, and specific 6-month notification requirement provision

upgraded_references.prior_citation: Canadian Environmental Protection Act and CEPA notification requirements

upgraded_citation.source_section: Part 5 — Controlling Toxic Substances (New Substances Notification)

key_provisions.section_81: Notification requirements for new substances — persons proposing to manufacture or import a substance not on the Domestic Substances List (DSL) must notify the Minister

upgraded_citation.implementing_regulations: New Substances Notification Regulations (Chemicals and Polymers), SOR/2005-247
upgraded_citation.pharmaceutical_pathway: Most drug substances are excluded from CEPA NSN requirements via Domestic Substances List (DSL) or via Schedule 2 exclusion (substances regulated under other federal acts including the Food and Drugs Act). For new APIs not on the DSL and not otherwise excluded, CEPA notification applies.
upgraded_citation.health_canada_guidance: Health Canada Guidance Document: Environmental Assessment of Drug Submissions (available via drugpr-medpreg.health-canada.ca)
upgraded_citation.source_page: S.C. 1999, c. 33, Part 5 (sections 80-89) — New Substances Notification
upgraded_citation.residual_risk: low — product-specific DSL status must be verified for each API; most pharmaceutical APIs are on DSL or otherwise exempt
upgraded_citation.annotation_status: ENHANCED — full statute citation (S.C. 1999, c. 33), key sections identified, DSL exclusion pathway documented

upgraded_references.precision_improvement: Added S.C. citation, Royal Assent date, specific CEPA sections (81, 82, 85), implementing regulations (SOR/2005-247), and clarified DSL exclusion pathway for most pharmaceutical APIs

summary.method: Statutory precision enhancement via standard legal indexing (US Code, Statutes at Large, Canadian Statutes)
summary.limitation_case: DIV-041 (state pharmacy laws) — state law specifics inherently vary across 50 states; federal framework fully cited; representative state examples provided
summary.recommendation: Add 21 USC Title 21 Chapter 9 (FD&C Act), FDAAA (PL 110-85), FDASIA (PL 112-144), FDORA (PL 117-328), and CEPA 1999 (S.C. 1999, c. 33) to stage-2 corpus to enable full automated statute-level tracing.
